data.title: 12% Dividend Yields Or Higher
data.description:
We’re covering a handful of stocks with yields of 12% or higher. For investors in the mortgage REIT or BDC sector, yields are typically pretty high. This time we’re focusing on some of the highest-yielding mortgage REITs. To get even more specific, we’re going to focus on agency mortgage REITs. We want to run a quick comparison for several of the mortgage REITs, but we also want to discuss the state of the industry.

The state of the industry: A bunch of agency mortgage REITs trade at very high price-to-book ratios. That's bad. OK, check off discussing state of the industry. Now we can talk about the individual mortgage REITs.

CHMI

Tiny REIT. Sometimes Cherry Hill Mortgage (CHMI) it can be a good bargain for trading. Big dividends, but constantly losing value. No, really. They lost a ton of value.

Shares are trading a bit below our estimate of book value, but not all that far. I wonder if CHMI will look for an opportunity to issue equity. Yes, it would probably be dilutive to book value.

As a mortgage REIT shrinks, it becomes more difficult to cover the overhead costs. Since CHMI is so small and pays out so much capital to shareholders, it’s very difficult to maintain book value. One way to avoid having to sell the mortgage REIT (which can make executives sad) is to simply issue more shares to the public.

CHMI's strategy includes MSRs (mortgage-servicing rights).

There are some other mortgage REITs that have used MSRs to pull off a much better performance than many of the mortgage REITs. CHMI just isn’t part of that club. CHMI has a 16.5% dividend yield.

NLY

Annaly Capital Management (NLY) is the biggest of the mortgage REITs. Like AGNC, they also have a material premium to our estimate of current book value. As of 2/19/2025, we estimated the price-to-current book around 1.09x. That’s horrible. It’s smaller than AGNC, but it's still too much. The dividend yield comes in at 12%, which is the lowest among the agency mortgage REITs and the lowest we’re going to cover in this article. There’s nothing fundamentally wrong with NLY. I wouldn’t mind owning NLY, if I could buy it at a materially lower price. But I sure wouldn’t be excited to hold onto it here.

AGNC

AGNC Investment Corp. (AGNC) is the second largest among mortgage REITs. Among agency mortgage REITs, there are only two where the common equity market capitalization is greater than $2 billion.

Besides being big, AGNC trades at astounding price-to-book ratios. We’ve been harping on this for a while, but it bears repeating. Investors are paying a massive premium to book value. That premium is bigger than an entire year of dividends. Who wants to pay that? Yuck. You can be an income investor or a total return investor, but don’t be a bad investor. Yeah, the 13.7% dividend yield looks nice and the earnings are even higher, but the way mortgage REITs blend historical amortized costs and hedge accounting can lead investors to misunderstand what the earnings actually mean.

ARR

ARMOUR Residential REIT (ARR), the REIT that needs to put its entire first name in caps lock, is trading around our estimate for book value. Is that a reasonable price? I think it’s too high. Why? Because ARR’s history of managing the portfolio is dreadful. Returns are so bad, management could’ve just invested all the capital into the iShares MBS ETF (MBB), paid themselves a salary, overpaid the dividend, and they still could’ve depleted book value slower.

ORC

Orchid Island Capital (ORC) has a premium to projected book value. Are you kidding me? ORC’s price moves down. That’s what it does. It’s kind of like the reverse Bitcoin. Not in any fundamental sense, but the price has declined massively over the years. Does ORC do anything really unique? Not really. There's nothing proprietary about their model. They buy some agency MBS. They put on some hedges. They pay out a huge dividend. Most quarters they announce book value declined. From time to time they announce how much they are cutting the future dividends.

All The Stocks

The charts compare the following companies and their preferred shares or baby bonds:

  • BDCs: (CSWC), (BXSL), (TSLX), (OCSL), (GAIN), (TPVG), (FSK), (MAIN), (ARCC), (GBDC), (OBDC), (SLRC)

  • Commercial mREITs: (GPMT), (FBRT), (BXMT)

  • Residential Hybrid mREITs: (MITT), (CIM), (RC), (MFA), (EFC), (NYMT),

  • Residential Agency mREITs: (NLY), (AGNC), (CHMI), (DX), (TWO), (ARR), (ORC)

  • Residential Originator and Servicer mREITs: (RITM), (PMT)

Easier Charts for Seeking Alpha Readers

You know what I don’t like to do? Deal with uploading and sourcing images.

If you’re looking for the charts that normally go in this series:

Click here for all the charts. (No paywall, that would be dumb).

I’ll consider reader feedback and may make further changes. Your feedback is appreciated!

Another potential option would be simply linking directly to some Google Sheets I run for the series. That would also give me the option to add in additional data fields as requested by readers.

Happy to hear any suggestions from our readers.

This method makes the entire article shorter and if someone really wants to save a tiny bit of data, they can do that.

Thank You

data.title: Yield Hunting Part 10: 8%-Plus YTM With MidCap Financial And Its Baby Bond MFICL
data.description:
Today, we will continue the yield hunting and analyze another low-duration option: MidCap Financial Investment Corporation (MFIC) and its 8% baby bond MFICL. But before presenting our idea, we would like to show you where bond yields are at the moment.

Treasury yield curve.

The curve finally looks somewhat normal after a long period of inverted yields. If you look closer at the absolute values of the yields, you may still view the curve as flat.

Investment-grade historical spreads.

The chart below shows how narrow investment grade spreads are compared to the five-year treasury yield:

High-yield historical spreads.

The picture here is very similar:

High-yield bonds have also narrowed their spread from the historical averages, and we are very close to the historical lows.

SPY Earnings Yield vs. 10-year treasury yield.

Bond treasury yields and equity yields are almost inverted right now. This is based on upward expectations for the earnings growth ratio for the companies in the index. If you choose the same old diversified average portfolios, you may end up buying most asset classes at record-high valuations. This is why we monitor any single deviation in fixed-income products, and we want every single product in our portfolio to have "alpha." Now, let's take a close look at the BDC company that we will discuss today.

MFIC Overview.

MidCap Financial Investment Corporation (NASDAQ: MFIC) is an externally managed, publicly traded, Business Development Company, focused on providing senior debt solutions to middle-market companies. We derive significant benefits from our affiliation with Apollo, a global high-growth alternative asset manager, and its unique partnership with MidCap Financial, a leading middle-market lender. Through MFIC, shareholders can access institutional quality private credit at an industry-leading fee structure.

MFIC had around $1.3B market capitalization. It manages nearly $3B in total assets, distributed in 169 holdings. The fund had 55.31% leverage, an expense ratio of 14.54%, and a non-leverage expense ratio of 1.51%. The weighted average debt investment yield is 11.6%.

BDC bonds yield curve.

Since we're going to discuss one of MFIC's bonds - MFICL, we will provide you with a few charts that can give you a general idea of ​​the sector. In the chart below, we can see MFICL does not belong with the best OTC BDC bonds in the sector:

And this is the comparison with the other OTC bonds:

In the chart below, you can see bonds that trade on the exchange (meaning they have the usual call option two years after the IPO) and are priced above par with yields to call above 5%. MFICL has a call date of 0.84 years and a yield to call or yield to worst of 7.5%, a maturity date of 3.84 years, and a yield to maturity of 8.06%.

Here, you can see bonds that are trading below par, with their yields and years to maturities, compared with MFICL:

The basic information about baby bond MFICL can be seen in the picture below:

At the time of writing, MFICL is trading at $25.5, that is, above its par value. Since the call date is relatively soon, it's important to know your yield if the company decides to repurchase MFICL in the future. Here’s what the stock’s Yield to Call (YTC) curve looks like right now:

Credit Score.

Let's take a look at the credit score of the fund. We will use Moody's Methodology. The basic metrics for determining credibility are the following:

In the chart below, we can look at all the BDC companies and their credit scores based on our adjusted methodology:

Profitability

Before we start, we want to let the readers know that we use the information from Q3 to determine the credit score since the company's annual report has not yet been released. We do not strictly adhere to Moody's formula, but we are guided by it. For example, we like to measure the company’s performance by taking the three-year return on net asset value (NAV), annualizing it, and then dividing it by the company’s average managed assets. In this case, we end up with a six score, the equivalent of which is an A2, one of the highest scores.

Leverage (Asset Coverage Ratio Cushion)

The company's leverage, or its Asset Coverage Ratio Cushion (ACRC), is the second parameter, and it has the highest weight when determining the overall credit score. The regulatory limit for asset coverage ratio is 150%. The higher - the better. For MFIC, the asset coverage ratio is approximately 180%. The asset coverage ratio cushion amounts to 21% - or a 10 score, which is equivalent to Baa3.

Asset quality

MFIC’s problem loans account for 1.5% of the portfolio, which is not relatively low compared to its peers. This is a nine score or a Baa2. Senior secured loans represent roughly 92.1% of the portfolio which gives us an Aa2.

Debt Structure

We use this metric to express how MFIC's financial managers spread the company's debt throughout the years:

This company has been around since 2004, and we expected a better distribution of its debt, but let's not forget that its holdings are less than the sector's benchmark holdings. Here, we take a score of 14, or B1. Below, you can see how it compares to the benchmark (ARCC) in the sector:

MFIC's secured debt represents 30.84% ​​of the portfolio. Moody's rates are such a ratio of 10 or Baa3. The company's overall credit score is 9.6, or a Baa3 rating. We also adjust the credit score by considering factors such as market capitalization, premium to NAV, historical yield spreads, and number of holdings in the portfolio. After assessing all of these elements, MFIC has a final score of 9.81, which is equivalent to Baa3.

Sector comparison.

Let’s concentrate on the bond spread percentile for the whole sector. We have rated all of the companies based on the metrics we used in this article.

We use the best bonds of each company in the sector. In the chart above, you can see how each company's spread percentile rank compares to its credit rating percentile rank. If the blue line is higher than the red line, we are earning more than we are risking. The opposite is also true: If the red line is higher than the blue line, we are risking too much for what we are earning. In our case, MFIC sits in a good spot among its peers, and we can see that its credit score and yield are at the same level. There's no significant alpha in MFICL, and we view it as a normally priced bond. It's added with a lower weight in the portfolio. and we are monitoring for reaching par.

Conclusion.

At Trade With Beta, we're looking for low-duration products with high yields and decent credit quality, and MFICL fits all the criteria. At times, they offer the chance to generate yields above 8%, and we take those. At the time of writing, we will be more than happy if MFICL is redeemed and we end up earning a 7.5% yield to call.

data.title: KE Holdings: A Data-Driven Powerhouse Reshaping China's Real Estate Landscape
data.description:
We are bullish on KE Holdings Inc. (NYSE:BEKE) as China’s property market is showing incremental signs of stabilization. While we acknowledge that China’s housing market is likely to remain challenging given that primary and secondary home sales continue to decline and that the overall consumption sentiment remains weak, we feel that this backdrop presents an attractive opportunity to invest in quality companies with a competitive moat at a reasonable valuation.

We consider BEKE to be such a company for several reasons:

First, BEKE has an unparalleled competitive moat that delivers a flywheel effect to drive many of its businesses. We note that BEKE’s online and offline platforms position the company as a clear leader in real estate transactions/brokerage and other home value-added services. This is translating into a positive flywheel effect as BEKE can expand its non-brokerage businesses into other value-add areas such as renovation, furnishing, and rental. This implies that BEKE has a superior flywheel effect similar to the one we have seen in Xiaomi (see: Xiaomi: Charging Ahead In The Ecosystem Race With A Spinning Flywheel)

Second, the data-driven residential platform Beihaojia could reshape China’s residential development industry. We note that Beihaojia uses big data and a C2M model to develop homes based on buyer demand, shifting the market to a buyer-driven model. Pilot projects in Chengdu and Xi’an aim to shorten transaction cycles and optimize supply-demand matching. If successful, we think it could revolutionize China’s housing market by improving efficiency and reducing developer risks.

Finally, the current valuation looks appealing with an attractive upside. Given the 12% projected revenue and 17% earnings growth outlook, the valuation looks compelling at 1.2x forward EV/Revenue or 14.8x forward P/E.

We value BEKE at 1.5x forward revenue multiple given that we consider the company to remain a growth company with a significant moat and should be valued closer to that of its global peers, such as Compass and Redfin, as a real estate brokerage platform but also closer to its domestic internet peers such as Meituan and Alibaba that largely operate on a commission-based model that is heavily tied to the health of the Chinese economy. Our valuation multiple is reasonable given that our target implies an 18x forward earnings multiple, in line with Chinese large-cap internet peers.

A strong economic moat with a flywheel effect

Scale matters in property brokerage, and we see BEKE certainly having this supply-side advantage of over 46k active stores and 423k active agents across the country, which generated over US$100bn in total GTV in the last quarter.

The key to enabling such scale lies in BEKE’s proprietary agent cooperation network, or ACN, which works similarly to the MLS platform used by brokers outside of China. We believe this drives a strong economic moat for BEKE and creates a strong barrier to entry for the other brokerage platforms.

ACN enhances collaboration among real estate brokers, increases transaction efficiency, and improves industry transparency. Unlike traditional real estate brokerage models, where brokers often compete against each other and prioritize individual commissions, BEKE’s ACN fosters cooperation by allowing multiple brokers to participate in different transaction stages and share commissions accordingly. This transforms brokers from competitors into workmates, significantly improving the sector's overall efficiency.

Ultimately, the ACN platform lowers the entry barrier for new brokers and results in higher agent retention, as many brokers can be involved in various stages of the transaction. More importantly, this structure creates a better compensation model for the brokers, which increases the platform's stickiness.

The supply-side advantage in stores and agents coupled with the leading properties listing service, ACN, are driving that demand-side growth algorithm as indicated by the growing transaction volume and the emergence of non-brokerage businesses such as renovation furnishing, and home rental that has seen strong revenue growth in recent quarters.

Revolutionize China’s residential development?

BEKE introduced a new business arm, Beihaojia, that leverages big data insights to acquire land and develop residential properties under a C2M model. Given BEKE's abundant data on consumer preferences and demand profiles, the rationale is that BEKE could use big data to analyze buyers' needs and price expectations and develop properties based on buyers’ needs.

Compared with conventional property development, BEKE is looking to address the longer transaction cycle that is characterized by the current market landscape and the inaccurate manual feasibility study conducted by the developers. The end goal is to transform the residential properties market into a buyers' market.

BEKE has acquired land in Chengdu and Xi’an to test this concept. While this initiative remains in its early days, we believe that it could revolutionize China’s residential housing market by more efficiently pairing demand and supply. This would minimize the discount risk to the developers and drive more efficient use of resources amid China’s aging and shrinking population and declining residential housing demand.

Valuation looks appealing with an attractive upside

BEKE is currently trading at 1.3x forward EV/Revenue multiple and 17x forward earnings multiple. There is a debate on how BEKE should be valued on a comparable basis. One would argue that BEKE should be valued closer to real estate brokerage peers such as Redfin and Compass, given that it generates 80% of its revenue from primary and secondary housing transactions, while some argue that its internet-based business model should value it closer to its domestic internet peers.

We took a blended approach when looking at BEKE. While we acknowledge that the company operates mainly within the property sector, which is likely to remain depressed in the foreseeable future, we also recognize that its internet-based transaction-driven revenue model mirrors that of other transaction-based internet platforms such as Meituan, Ctrip, and even Alibaba given its business nature is heavily susceptible to China’s macro backdrop similar to that of the other internet companies.

Redfin and Compass trade between 1x and 2x forward revenue multiples, while the Chinese internet platforms trade around 2x, with the exception of Ctrip, which trades closer to 5x, and Tencent, which trades closer to 6x.

We believe that BEKE deserves a higher revenue multiple than its foreign peers, given its higher reliance on the internet-based model and a higher multiple than its domestic peers due to its higher revenue growth outlook.

The stock has been trading between 1x and 2x forward revenue over the past 3 years, and if we were to assign a conservative 1.5x forward revenue multiple, then this would imply $23/share, or an 18% upside from the current level.

At 1.5x forward revenue multiple, the valuation implies 18x forward earnings, which, we believe, is reasonable as it is largely in line with that of large-cap internet peers such as Ctrip, Tencent, and Meituan.

There are several key risks to our thesis:

On the macro side, China’s property sector has yet to find a bottom, and we could see prices trend lower as we head into the year given that the early signs of stabilization in housing prices at the beginning of the year could be driven by developers are still maintaining a wait-and-see attitude on the demand. Given the lack of material stimulus measures to jumpstart the housing market and the existing policies such as lower down payments and mortgage rates, which have yet to translate to sustained demand generation, the deflationary spiral could be ongoing for some time before it stabilizes.

On the micro side, BEKE has been expanding the number of stores to maximize market share capture, and this has seen solid progress in recent quarters. However, we would be skeptical of this trajectory if the macro overhang worsens and ROI and productivity per store could face dilution risk.

Finally, BEKE recently added Beihaojia as a new business line focused on data-driven residential development services. The company has purchased land in both Xi’an and Chengdu to execute this C2M residential development strategy. While we believe this is a novel approach to property development, we are concerned about the overcapacity challenge within China’s property market and whether moving into residential property development, as novel as it may seem, is the right strategic decision.

On the flip side, we would be more bullish on BEKE if it leverages its data-driven insights to disrupt the overseas market, similar to what PDD (see: PDD: Navigating Tariff Uncertainty, Driving Long-Term Growth Through Global Expansion) and Meituan (see: Meituan: Capturing The Lens Of Opportunity) have done with visible successes, as we feel that BEKE could address some of the gaps that ex-China market presents due to lack of internet-based model.

data.title: FLCA: Why I Prefer Canada Stock-Picking To This ETF
data.description:
I have always been interested in Canada. Very early in my career, I was a daily reader of a Canadian daily newspaper, and I became fairly familiar with the blue chip stocks there. That was a long time ago, but my interest has been rekindled. That’s in part due to the presence of some great subscribers to my investing group here, Sungarden YARP Portfolio, who are in Canada and have asked about stocks and ETFs there.

Because while the US and Canada are so much alike (and no, I’m not referring to that whole “51st State” chatter), their markets are vastly different when it comes to things like sector allocation. And perhaps more importantly at some future time, there’s a different currency. So, my interest in the Canada stock market, which primarily trades on the Toronto Stock Exchange (TSX) is both at the company level and also at the currency/macro level. I have a pet theory, which I have discussed with my investing group, that at some point the US Dollar will be seen as more vulnerable, given our partisan politics amid a historic debt level.

Should that occur, investors will be running all over the place looking for non-US dollar investments, or to at least have some non-US dollar currency exposure. ETFs provide an easy way to be multi-currency. And many also allow investors to access the returns of stocks in non-US markets which, when translated back to the US dollar, can be worth more than their local values if the US dollar has dipped versus those currencies.

I am explaining this at a very basic level because we US-domiciled investors have not really had to concern ourselves with this aspect of investing for a very long time. Since the start of 2021, the year after the pandemic hit, this ETF tracking the US dollar versus a trade-weighted basket of currencies is up 34%. That’s a lot for a single currency. And perhaps not sustainable.

That implies that anything non-US in the equity, bond or even straight currency market is at least more interesting than it has been in a few years. So here, I’ll focus on an ETF that provides access to stocks of companies based Canada. While I conclude that I’m neutral on Franklin Templeton ETF Trust (NYSEARCA:FLCA), I do expect to drill down (not a Canada mining pun) into some stocks from that nation in the near future. But at the country index level, I’m currently less enthusiastic. I see a lot of offsetting behavior, such that winners and losers (forward-looking) are liable to cancel each other out too much. For what its worth, I have the same opinion of the S&P 500.

FLCA: a competitive Canada stock ETF

Franklin Resources’ Templeton unit is an international equity giant for decades. It has been active in Canada for a very long time. So management and understanding the nuances of non-US investing are not an issue here. The fund is 7 years old, has a skinny expense ratio and enough assets to be considered a peer of the 2 other Canada ETFs that trade on US exchanges.

Here are the 3, side by side. The bottom line is they all move in sync. So deciding which one an investor chooses is not likely to be based on past performance. For me, that is rarely a priority in my analysis anyway. I’m not concerned with what I missed, but rather what I can make while managing risk going forward.

FLCA has about 50 stocks, and doesn’t change more than a few each year. But as is the case with most ETFs I favor, it is concentrated at the top, with nearly half of assets in the top 10 stocks by weight.

This (below) is what I alluded to earlier. That sector allocation is very different from the US, where technology dominates, and healthcare and consumer sectors play a big role too. Here, we see that financial stocks dominate, at more than 40% weighting. Tech is somewhat average weight, with energy the second biggest portion. That makes sense given the natural resources present in Canada. Basic Materials thus have a noticeable role here at 9%, versus next to nothing in the S&P 500 index.

I will only allude to my opinion that stock selection is likely to be my preferred path in Canada, rather than an ETF at this point. That could change if FLSA really tanks. But that’s not yet the case. What remains to be seen (by me and my subscribers, at least) is whether the best opportunities are in the big names here, or in smaller big caps and mid/small cap stocks.

The top 10 is 40% banks (4 stocks), so in line with the full portfolio. And below, expanding to the top 25, you can see why I am looking at stock picking here. Those valuations, particularly on trailing earnings, are a decent discount to their US counterparts. Again, I’ll be doing more work on this and reporting it, first to my group, then to the broader Seeking Alpha audience. And similar to the US market, there are lots of highly appreciated stocks here. Finding value is not easy today, apart from maybe segments of Europe and Asia.

What I see in this chart below is that the banks are not dominating performance on a contribution basis, at least for the past 12 months.

That 19x earnings doesn’t sound cheap, but consider that the US/S&P 500 is about a 50% premium to that. However, this has a lot to do with the high valuations of tech stocks, and the fact that Canada’s stock market is not nearly as driven by them. And, Canada tech stocks are not the big giant dominators we have in the US.

Yield is not bad here if my assumption is the whole global equity market will fall hard at some point in the next 1-2 years (I think it will). Because a “garden variety” 30% drop in global stock markets would push FLCA’s yield over 3%. The S&P 500 yields around 1.1% by comparison.

FLCA: not a buy, but a great shopping list

So my conclusion is that this and perhaps the other 2 Canada ETFs are a nice place to find a collection of names I can then run through my usual process, fundamentally and technically. If and when I find attractive names, I’ll highlight some in future reports.

data.title: Valvoline: Gearing Up, Pursuing Continued Growth
data.description:
In November, I believed that the growth engine of Valvoline Inc. (VVV) was not fully lubricated itself. Its transition to becoming a smaller, yet faster-growing business following the 2022 sale of the Global Products business, has been awarded with higher valuations, yet I still had reservations on the investment case.

Ever since the company has largely resorted to buybacks to grow the piece of the pie to investors. However, at the start of 2025, Valvoline is making a substantial acquisition in its attempt to grow the business materially as well.

This means that real growth is seen, yet at premium valuation multiples in the low twenties, with quite some debt taken on and questions on the long-term positioning to be asked, I continue to approach the situation with caution.

Vehicles Taken Care Of

Valvoline is a well-known name in the automotive care sector. The company describes itself as a vehicle care business which is powering the future of mobility. The company does this by providing oil, lubricants as well as gear, transmission, and radiators.

The company used to be part of Ashland Inc. (ASH), but it was spun off from this business in 2015. The resulting business generated some $3 billion in sales in 2021, while it posted operating earnings of around half a billion, resulting in adjusted earnings of around $2 per share.

At the time, the company comprised a $1.8 billion Global Products business, which posted segment earnings of $300 million. The other was a $1.2 billion retail segment, which posted similar operating profits (in dollar terms), ahead of corporate cost allocation.

Following the sale of the Global Products business in a $2.65 billion deal to Saudi Aramco, in which net proceeds were seen at just $2.25 billion, the company has undergone a massive transition.

Forwarding to 2023, the company posted a 17% increase in annual sales to $1.44 billion as operating profits were reported at $264 million, with earnings reported at $1.23 per share. Earnings took a beating, following the foregone earnings power following the divestment of the Global Products business, not being offset by huge and aggressive share buybacks.

About 2024

The company initially guided for 2024 same-store sales to be up some 6-9%, with revenues seen up to $1.6-$1.7 billion, and earnings seen up to $1.40-$1.65 per share.

The company quickly cut the higher end of the full-year sales guidance by fifty million, as it embarked on fairly aggressive share buybacks to keep earnings per share intact. Fast forwarding to November of last year, the company posted a 12% increase in 2024 sales to $1.62 billion, with adjusted earnings reported at $1.57 per share.

Net debt of $1.03 billion resulted in a leverage ratio of 2.3 times based on EBITDA reported at $443 million. The 2025 guidance came in a bit soft, however, if you ask me. The company guided for 2025 sales at a midpoint of $1.70 billion. Total growth was seen at around 5%, despite another 160–185 stores added to the systemwide franchise of the business. This adds to a store count of some 2,000 stores, split pretty evenly between franchise and company-owned stores.

Adjusted earnings were seen up just 5 cents to a midpoint of $1.62 per share. Earnings per share growth was held back by lower share buyback activity, in part as leverage was moving up a bit and capital spending of nearly a quarter of a billion put pressure on free cash flow generation. Earnings per share growth was furthermore expected to be pressured amidst cost inflation, as well as higher collection reimbursement payments related to oil collection.

Trading at $38 in November, I failed to see the appeal at 23–24 times forward adjusted earnings, while debt was quite high already. This was certainly the case, as I furthermore have doubts about the true long-term positioning of the business, geared so much to ICE vehicles.

Trading Flat

Since November, shares have traded in a relatively tight $35-$40 range. Currently trading at $38 per share, shares trade at par compared to the levels at which shares traded back in November.

Early in February, Valvoline reported its first quarter results for the fiscal year 2025. Total sales were reported up some 11% to $414 million, with systemwide same-store sales growth reported at a decent 8%. Adjusted earnings improved three pennies to $0.32 per share, as the solid start to the year made it easy for management to reiterate its confidence in the full-year outlook.

Net debt was reported at $973 million, following solid cash flow generation, as leverage ratios come in the low 2s. The 130 million shares of Valvoline give equity of the business a $5.0 billion equity valuation, and the company a near $6.0 billion enterprise valuation.

Recently, Valvoline announced a $625 million cash deal to acquire Breeze Autocare. The company mostly operates under the Oil Changers brand, operating 200 (all company-owned) stores across some 17 states, including California, Texas, and the Midwest. With a $200 million revenue contribution, the deal is valued at 3.1 times sales, while Valvoline´s enterprise valuation is equal to 3.5 times sales.

Valued at a 10.7 times EBITDA multiple, the EBITDA contribution is seen around $58 million, with the deal set to be neutral to earnings per share in year one post-closing. On a pro forma basis, the company is on track to generate just over half a billion in EBITDA, needed as pro forma net debt is seen around $1.6 billion, for a leverage ratio at, or just above, 3 times.

What Now?

The Breeze Autocare deal will grow Valvoline by around 10%, perhaps a fraction more, as leverage ratios will increase about a turn. All this is positive, in combination with a solid start to the year, but I continue to be wary of the valuation.

With Valvoline Inc. stock trading at a continued 22–23 times elevated forward earnings multiple, I am taking it easy given the positioning questions and leverage employed.

Given all this, I think that this deal looks relatively compelling, but that to an important extent is the result of a higher standalone valuation of Valvoline itself. Hence, I find myself repeating the conclusion from November, after a relatively large and relatively appealing deal, as overall valuations are too demanding for me.

data.title: Finding Value Off The Beaten Path With George Risk Industries
data.description:
There are very few individual stocks in the broader market that currently interest me in the mid-cap and large-cap sectors. Valuations in the US market have been stretched significantly over the last 12 months, and numerous other authors on Seeking Alpha have expressed the need for caution. Because I think the overall market is overvalued, I have been looking for stocks that are trading out of the spotlight, off the beaten path, so to speak. When I screen for new stock ideas, I often include a criteria for low trading volume, while searching for companies with growing earnings and low or no debt. One stock that clearly fits that description is George Risk Industries (OTCPK:RSKIA).

After further review of the company's balance sheet and quarterly reports, I believe George Risk Industries represents a compelling value with a PE ratio below 10, no debt on its books, and a rather interesting investment portfolio that is worth almost half of the company's overall market cap. I am giving a buy rating to RSKIA with some added cautions that should be included when investing in a thinly traded micro-cap stock.

Micro-Cap Stock Risks

Before I go into further detail about George Risk Industries, it is important to note that this investment idea carries added risk because of the size of the company and the low trading volume of its stock. The company currently has a market cap of $84 million. The average daily trading volume for the stock is under 2000 shares, and it is not uncommon for RSKIA to have no trading volume on any given day.

When purchasing any thinly traded stock like George Risk Industries, a market order to purchase the stock could lead the buyer to pay significantly more than the previous trading price. The spread between the bid and the ask for RSKIA can approach $1.00 or even more at times, even though the stock itself currently trades for only $17 per share. A market order to purchase even a few hundred shares could move the stock price by 5% or more in a single transaction. Limit buy orders are strongly recommended for any transaction in this security to avoid creating a significant change in the stock price.

Company Overview

George Risk Industries was founded in 1965 and has been family-owned since its beginnings. Headquartered in Kimball, Nebraska, the company is currently run by CEO Stephanie Risk-McElroy, the daughter of the former CEO, Ken Risk, and granddaughter of the company's namesake.

The company designs and produces a variety of electronic components related to a wide range of functions including home and business security, water detection, and wire installation. It's most profitable business segment involves the production of various security alarm products. Its clients include individuals and businesses, as well as government entities both in the US and worldwide. While all of this may not sound like the typical modern-day growth story, the company is remarkably profitable with above average margins, as can be seen from the financial data given later in this article.

When it comes to financial reporting, the company certainly is not spending much money on marketing or graphic design. Visitors to the "Investor Relations" section of the company website who are looking for quarterly reports are sent to links on the SEC.gov website. There are no conference calls or PowerPoint slides to guide investors through the highs and lows of the most recent quarter. There are no ratings for the stock from any investment companies. This is part of the reason that I believe the stock currently trades at such a significant discount.

RSKIA Financial Metrics

Financial analysis of George Risk Industries reveals a number of impressive results. The stock trades at a significant discount to the overall market from a price-to-earnings ratio perspective. Yet, the company does not appear to be a stagnant value trap, as it has strong profit margins and growing earnings. RSKIA currently has a PEG ratio of under 0.5.

GEORGE RISK INDUSTRIES
PE Ratio 8.2
Net Profit Margin 45.4%
Cash Flow Margin 36.96%
Return on Equity 19.5%
PE Growth Ratio 0.48
Long Term Debt 0

Any one of these data points could be reason for further investigation into the company, but the collection of all six with a long history of profits seem too good to ignore. In the last RSKIA annual report, CEO Stephanie Risk-McElroy reported that net sales for the last fiscal year were "$21,767,000, up 8.95% over the previous fiscal year. Net income for the year ended April 30, 2024 was $7,558,000, a 58.88% increase from the reported net income of $4,757,000 for the year ended April 30, 2023. Earnings per common share (“EPS”) for the years ended April 30, 2024 and 2023 was at $1.54 per share and $0.96 per share, respectively." Earnings growth of 60% per year should not be expected to repeat itself on a regular basis, but it is clear that this company is operating from a position of strength, and with no debt on the books, leadership is running a fiscally responsible operation.

Another positive to note in the year ahead is that the products made by George Risk Industries are all made in the US. Therefore, most of their sales should be shielded from the concerns with various tariffs that may be implemented by the current presidential administration. International sales, however, could be affected by tariffs imposed by other countries.

RSKIA Investments and Cash on Hand

While business at George Risk Industries seems to be humming along, there is more to this company than meets the eye. Perhaps the most interesting thing about George Risk Industries is the size of the investment account currently reflected on its balance sheet. Almost half of the company's market cap is represented by these assets.

Here is the way that the company describes these investments on its last quarterly report:

The Company has investments in publicly traded equity securities, state and municipal debt securities, real estate investment trusts, and money markets. The investments in debt securities, which include municipal bonds and bond funds, mature between August 2025 and December 2050.

These investments are all managed by a third-party advisor. Stocks and bonds are actively traded in the market. The portfolio increased by just over 5% during this six month period shown below, at a time when the S&P 500 was returning 11%. The under-performance is somewhat expected given the allocation to bonds, REITS, and CD's. However, these more conservative investments should also protect the account in the event of a market decline.

If you add the $36,359,000 in investments from the end of October to the additional $5,454,000 that the company held in cash at that time, and the market cap of $84 million is almost 50% composed of non-business related assets. That price to earnings ratio of 8.2 mentioned earlier would fall below 5 if these assets were excluded from consideration.

One could question whether there are better ways to manage this unique investment portfolio. Could the assets be liquidated and distributed to shareholders? (There are just under five million shares outstanding, so a liquidation could lead to a payout of over $8 per share to each shareholder.) Should the investment approach be more aggressive to increase its growth potential or more income focused to increase the dividend? These are valid questions, but the fact remains that all of the positive metrics from its actual business operations that were mentioned earlier look even more impressive when considering these assets. Should the company give more clear direction on its plans for this investment portfolio, I could be inclined to give this stock an even stronger buy recommendation.

RSKIA Shows Outstanding Dividend Growth

Since 2009, George Risk Industries has raised its annual dividend every year. In 2012, it did pay an additional special dividend that makes it look like the dividend decreased the following year, but the standard annual payout has been on an upswing for 14 straight years. Since 2021, the annual dividend has increased 100%, including a 54% increase over the last year when the payout improved from $0.65 to $1.00. Over the last decade, the compound average growth rate of the payout from RSKIA is just over 12%. The stock receives an “A+” rating in every category from Dividend.com.

The current dividend yield for the stock is 5.88%, and dividends are only paid once a year in the fall. When compared to other stocks in its sector, RSKIA is far and above the sector median in all categories related to dividend growth on Seeking Alpha.

Given the increases in the dividend stretch back to 2009, there is no reason to believe that payouts will not continue to increase. The company generated more than three times the profits to cover the $1.00 per share dividend in 2024.

There Are Other Risks to Consider

In addition to the risks discussed at the beginning of this article that are associated with any micro-cap stock, RSKIA carries some added concerns. Roughly 60% of the outstanding stock is owned by members of the Risk family. Therefore, other shareholders do not have much of a say in how the company is run or what the company may choose to do with the cash that it generates on a regular basis. While RSKIA has established a clear track record of healthy dividend increases over an extended period of time, these dividends also create tax consequences for the Risk family as they are receiving most of these payouts.

I have looked back at the insider holdings for the company dating back to 2000, and I cannot find one instance where a family member sold their stock in the open market. All insider transactions posted are from gifts or through inheritance after the death of a family member. In short, the family ownership of this company is not likely to change anytime soon.

There is another rather unique risk to consider listed at the end of the company's last quarterly report. Toward the end of the filing, the company reported the following:

The small size of our Company limits our ability to achieve the desired level of separation of duties for proper internal controls and financial reporting, particularly as it relates to financial reporting to assure material disclosures or implementation of newly issued accounting standards are included. We have hired a Controller, and a secondary review of annual and quarterly filings does occur with an outside CPA. However, the current CEO and CFO roles are being fulfilled by the same individual and we do not have an audit committee. We do not believe we have met the full requirement for separation of duties for financial reporting purposes.

The long history of the company gives me reason to believe that this is not a significant concern, but I bring it to light to emphasize the uniqueness of this stock and a potential risk that certainly would not exist at a larger company.

The Bottom Line

George Risk Industries is not a company in which you would want to invest a significant share of your portfolio because of its low trading volume and the higher risk involved in investing in micro-cap stocks. However, the financial metrics for RSKIA are certainly worthy of closer attention. Small, limit-order buys are the best way to approach an investment here.

The stock will likely have large, irregular moves in the short-term because of the wide gap in the spread between the bid and the ask on any given day. Over the long run, however, this company looks like a winner for both capital growth and dividend income. I feel confident in giving RSKIA a buy rating for investors who are risk tolerant and willing to stand through the potential for short-term price swings.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
